text: The robotic surgical setup includes four arms: the ProGrasp in the left arm, the Maryland Dissector in the second arm, a camera, and a hook cautery device, all visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon toggles between standard and immunofluorescence imaging to assess the anatomy, observing a dilated cystic duct and considering the possibility of a long infundibulum.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to gently manipulate and tease the cystic duct, toggling between views to achieve the critical view of safety.

The surgeon continues to manipulate the cystic duct, attempting to achieve the critical view of safety, while toggling between standard and immunofluorescence imaging to assess the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the cautery device to apply short bursts of energy on the crossing vessels, ensuring hemodynamic control during the robotic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon inspects the duct for the presence of stones, observing that no stones are visible within the duct.
The surgeon isolates the cystic artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to ensure a clear view for further dissection.
The surgeon visually inspects the duct for the presence of stones, ensuring no obstructions are visible.
The surgeon uses round tip scissors to manipulate tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully separate the gallbladder from the cystic plate, ensuring precise detachment from the underlying tissue.
